title: Interview Opening and Introductions
summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill opens the recording on 4 June 2011 in Albany, Georgia, with cameraman John Bishop at the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion. He introduces the Civil Rights History Project as a joint undertaking of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture and welcomes Rev. Charles Sherrod.
related_events: Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion 2011

title: Arrival in Albany From Rock Hill
summary: Sherrod recounts coming to Albany around October 1961 with the experience of Rock Hill, South Carolina behind him, where he had been jailed on the chain gang. He describes the eighth chapter of Romans as the conviction that carried him into Southwest Georgia.

title: Nothing Can Separate Me
summary: Sherrod explains the meaning the Rock Hill jail and chain gang gave to Romans 8, the simple verse that nothing can separate him from the love of God and Christ Jesus. He describes carrying that conviction to Albany as the belief that nothing but death could stop the mission of developing his people.

title: Indignities of Daily Jim Crow
summary: Sherrod describes how long it took to understand how to reach the elder who would not raise his eyes to a white man or woman. He recounts the indignity of Black women required to pull a stocking cap over their heads before trying on a hat, then paying with deference.

title: Existing Leadership Before SNCC
summary: Sherrod frames the challenge of breaking people away from fearing the sheriff and going down to the courthouse. He credits the leadership already present in Albany, including voter registration drives and the Criterion Club's petition to the City Commission for paved streets and better lighting.

title: Permission From the Adults
summary: Sherrod describes how he, Charles A. Jones, and Cordell Reagon obtained permission from the adults and the community to bring the movement into Albany. As organizers aged 19, 20, and 21, they turned first to young people, gathering small numbers in the churches that would have them.

title: Churches Open Under Threat
summary: Sherrod recalls young people coming to meetings in small churches, with the Presbyterian church in the city the first to open its doors. Other churches hesitated because of threats that any church hosting meetings would be burned to the ground, and some were.

title: Children Without the Fear
summary: Sherrod reflects on the problem of removing the fear that kept adults from standing up to the sheriff, police, and chief. The answer was the children, who had not been persecuted under the old order and could be taught nonviolence week after week against opponents who were looking for violence.

title: Recruiting the Four Gangs
summary: Sherrod describes confronting opponents with love and expecting peace, while still finding at least one switchblade knife whenever marchers lined up in the church. He recounts recruiting the east, west, north, and south side gangs to march nonviolently, drawing high school and state college students together for the campaign.

title: Testing the Bus Station
summary: Sherrod recalls that the first project was integrating the local bus station. He, Charles A. Jones, and Cordell tried it out first to confirm that arrests would follow, and found that they would.

title: First March Fills Two Churches
summary: Sherrod describes the strength people felt after their children were arrested, when the first march of about 255 people filled Shiloh Baptist Church and Mount Zion Baptist Church, facing each other, with thousands milling in the streets. He estimates two to three thousand people at that meeting.

title: Filling the County Jails
summary: Sherrod recounts the plan to keep demonstrating until the whole jail system was filled. When the Albany jail filled, authorities moved arrestees to Americus, Georgia and to Terrell, Baker, and Mitchell counties, the same counties the movement would later return to organize.

title: Heads Up, Still Loving
summary: Sherrod describes the joy of finding the same elders who once bent over with their heads down now speaking to white people without fear, trying on hats and picketing stores that would not change. He frames it as a great day for Albany, marching together while still loving in the face of hostility.

title: Decisions Through the Steering Committee
summary: Asked how he charted a course as the first field secretary, against Laurie Pritchett's tactic of dispersing prisoners across many county jails, Sherrod explains that he and the SNCC organizers did not make decisions alone. The Albany Movement steering committee made decisions together, and most strategy came out of that committee.

title: Picketing to Desegregate Stores
summary: Sherrod describes the first effort to desegregate all public accommodations by asking store owners to change their policies and picketing those who refused. He notes that incidents at a store, and stores with mostly Black customers but no Black workers on the register, drew the movement's attention.

title: Smith Grocery and the City Commission
summary: Sherrod recounts picketing Smith Grocery for two days until it closed, after which other stores opened their doors and began hiring against the wishes of the City Commission. He explains that the Commission wanted to keep segregation while business owners, feeling the loss, wanted to stop segregating their customers.

title: The Bus Boycott and City Transit
summary: Sherrod describes talking to the bus company, which was willing to integrate and hire Black drivers while the city blocked it. After a girl was thrown off a bus, no one rode, the movement started its own transportation system, and the city eventually began operating its own transit.

title: Baker County Voter Registration March
summary: Asked about a night of fear, Sherrod turns to Baker County, Georgia, where mass meetings and strategy came from the Baker County Movement. He describes a pleasant day with about 150 people lined up from the church toward the courthouse to demonstrate for the voting process before national news.

title: Beaten With Ax Handles
summary: Sherrod recounts seeing in his peripheral vision white young men with ax handles approaching the marchers as they walked toward the courthouse. He kept walking until blows struck his head, knocking him into the street and leaving him bleeding profusely as they beat him to the ground.

title: An Elder Shields His Body
summary: Sherrod credits an old woman trained in his nonviolent direct-action workshops, where he taught participants to throw their bodies across one another to share the blows. She screamed for the attackers to stop before they killed him, and that action saved his life until he was taken to the hospital.

title: Reflecting on the Reunion Week
summary: Asked how the weekend felt, Sherrod describes the joy of seeing people he had not seen in 50, 15, or 10 years. He shares the week's good news: signing contracts, securing a license for a radio station, and a New Communities Inc. land contract, blessings he says are too much goodness to bear.
related_events: Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion 2011

title: Closing Thanks and Archive Tag
summary: Mosnier thanks Reverend Sherrod for sitting down, calling it a privilege and honor. The recording closes with the standard tag identifying it as a presentation of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
